source.title: Combining Rational and Random Strategies in β-Glucosidase Zm-p60.1 Protein Library Construction

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study aimed to explore the effects of varying the highly conserved W373 position in β-glucosidase Zm-p60.1 using saturation mutagenesis, and to determine an optimal combination of rational and random strategies for constructing protein libraries with high fractions of positive hits. The initial hypothesis was that only a few variants would be active, but unexpectedly most variants showed activity, leading to a re-evaluation of library construction efficiency.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Saturation mutagenesis with degenerate primers was used to randomize position W373 in β-glucosidase Zm-p60.1. Unexpectedly, most variants were active, though with lower activity than wild type. The study concludes that a combination of randomized codon-based saturation mutagenesis and site-directed mutagenesis may be most efficient for libraries with high positive hit rates.
process_signals: Unexpected result: most variants active despite high conservation of W373
Shift in strategy: from expecting few hits to managing many hits
Conclusion: combined rational and random approach is efficient
metrics: Activity levels compared to wild type
failures_or_limits: Variants had significantly lower activity than wild type

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Designing randomized libraries requires balancing variability and screening effort. For cases with many positive hits, retaining variability is more important. W373 in Zm-p60.1 is highly conserved and important for substrate stabilization. Previous mutations (e.g., W373K) severely impaired activity, but a triple mutant partially rescued it. This study further explores W373 variation.
process_signals: Problem: how to efficiently construct libraries when many variants are active
Hypothesis: W373 is critical, so few active variants expected
Previous work: W373K inactive, but P372T/W373K/M376L partially active
failures_or_limits: Brassicaceae consensus cluster mutation abolishes activity

section_gists.heading: Results and Discussion
section_gists.gist: Bioinformatics analysis showed W373 is conserved but some variability exists. NNM codon randomization was used to generate variants excluding Trp and Met. Screening 21 clones found 20 active, with 10 different amino acids at position 373. The inactive clone had an additional stop codon. The observed amino acid frequencies correlated with codon usage. The high number of active variants prompted consideration of how to efficiently complete the library.
process_signals: Unexpected: 20/21 clones active, contrary to expectation of few hits
Observation: amino acid frequencies match codon probabilities
Decision: need to find all active variants, but random screening becomes inefficient
Shift: consider combining with site-directed mutagenesis to complete library
metrics: Amino acid frequencies: Leu (5), Pro (3), Arg (3), etc.
failures_or_limits: One inactive clone due to unintended stop codon at position 206
Low transformation efficiency limited initial library size
Random screening inefficient for finding remaining variants

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: Random mutagenesis becomes inefficient for finding new variants as library coverage increases. Combining randomized codon-based saturation mutagenesis with site-directed mutagenesis is beneficial for complete libraries. All expressed W373 variants were active, though with reduced activity, showing that variation at this conserved position is possible.
process_signals: Validation: combined strategy is efficient
Limitation: random screening hits diminishing returns
Key finding: all variants active, contradicting conservation-based expectations
failures_or_limits: Differences between predicted and observed frequencies due to random errors
Screening affected by expression levels, competency, colony mixing

section_gists.heading: Materials and Methods
section_gists.gist: This section is empty in the provided text.

section_gists.heading: Bioinformatics analysis
section_gists.gist: A set of 167 β-glycosidase sequences was aligned to assess variability at the position corresponding to W373. Monte Carlo simulations were used to calculate the average number of clones to screen for a new hit under different codon degeneration strategies. Cost-effectiveness of SDM vs. screening was compared.
process_signals: Rational design: bioinformatics to guide mutagenesis
Simulation to predict screening requirements
Cost analysis to choose strategy
metrics: Cost per variant: SDM ~100 USD, screening ~20 USD

section_gists.heading: Mutagenesis
section_gists.gist: NNM degenerate primers were used for saturation mutagenesis at W373 via QuikChange protocol. Low transformation efficiency resulted in few colonies. Missing variants were created by individual site-directed mutagenesis reactions. Expression problems with Leu and Ser variants required codon optimization.
process_signals: Constraint: low transformation efficiency limited library size
Revision: used SDM to create missing variants
Problem: expression issues with Leu and Ser, solved by codon change
methods: Individual SDM for missing variants (Cys, Glu, Ile, Met, Asn, Gln, Val, Tyr)
failures_or_limits: Low number of colonies due to low transformation efficiency
Expression problems with Leu and Ser variants

section_gists.heading: Screening
section_gists.gist: Activity screening was performed on Petri dishes using XGlc chromogenic substrate, with optimized conditions (40 mg/L XGlc, 37°C 12h then 16°C 12h).

section_gists.heading: Characterization of β-glucosidase Zm-p60.1 variants
section_gists.gist: Variants were expressed, purified, and tested for activity on natural substrate tZOG and artificial pNPG. Protein yield averaged 7.7 mg/L. All variants hydrolyzed substrates but at reduced levels.
process_signals: Validation: all variants active on both substrates
Partial result: activity reduced compared to wild type
methods: Activity assay with tZOG and pNPG

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Initial hypothesis: W373 is critical, so saturation mutagenesis will yield few active variants.
Unexpected observation: most variants are active, shifting focus to efficient library completion.
Revised strategy: combine random mutagenesis (NNM) with rational site-directed mutagenesis to cover all variants cost-effectively.
Validation: all 18 possible amino acid variants (excluding Met, Trp) were created and shown to be active, confirming that variation at W373 is tolerated.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What were the exact activity levels of each variant compared to wild type?
How did the authors decide which variants to create by SDM?
Were there any variants that could not be expressed or purified?
What were the results of the Monte Carlo simulation in terms of screening numbers?
How did the cost analysis influence the final strategy?

events.summary: Based on previous work, only one or a few functional variants of the weakly catalyzing mutant W373K were expected.
Sequencing of all 21 clones identified 10 variants at position 373, with frequencies correlating with codon usage.
numbers_or_metrics: Leu (5), Pro (3), Arg (3), Ser (2), Ala (2), Gly (2), His (1), Thr (1), Asp (1), Phe (1)

events.summary: One clone had no glucosidase activity due to an additional stop codon at position 206 introduced by an error in mutagenesis.
Computer simulations determined theoretical average numbers of clones needed to find new variants, showing a sharp increase for the last quarter of library construction.
Proposed ideal strategy combines random mutagenesis with site-directed mutagenesis to generate missing variants, due to high cost of completing library by random screening alone.

evidence_bank.quote: On the basis of our previous work we expected to find one or a few functional variants of the weakly catalyzing mutant W373K [7,8].
We identified 10 variants at the 373 position: Leu (five copies); Pro and Arg (each in three copies); Ser, Ala and Gly (each in two copies); His, Thr, Asp and Phe (all in one copy).
The clone with no glucosidase activity proved to be a proline variant with an additional stop codon introduced at position 206 by an unknown error in the mutagenesis procedure.
we determined theoretical average numbers of clones needed to find new variants and complete the library in computer simulations (Figure 2).
The simulations show that there is a sharp increase in numbers of clones needed for the last quarter of library construction
we propose that an ideal strategy should combine random mutagenesis with site-directed mutagenesis (SDM) to generate missing variants (Figure 3).

The simulation used to calculate theoretical average numbers of clones that needed to be screened for a new hit using each codon degeneration strategy was based on Monte Carlo method (scripts are enclosed as a File S6) implemented in Python 2.7.3 with 1 000 000 iterations simulating random clone picking.

The cost-effectiveness of the mutagenesis strategies was compared using estimates for (a) SDM and sequencing based on costs of commercial oligonucleotide synthesis + mutagenesis (by a QuikChange kit) + DNA isolation (by a Qiagen kit) + commercial sequencing (ca. 100 USD per variant in total), and b) screening only, based on DNA isolation (by a Qiagen kit) + commercial sequencing (ca. 20 USD per variant in total).
A library of Zm-p60.1 mutants with variations at the Trp373 position was created using the following set of primers introducing NNM degeneracy:
The number of colonies was low due to the low transformation efficiency of the engineered E. coli strain.
Finally, to complete the library we created missing variants using the QuikChange kit again in individual reactions with the same template and following primers –
Due to expression problems with Leu and Ser variants their codons were changed to TCC for Ser and CTG for Leu.
In surprising contrast to the high conservation at this position all enzyme variants were able to hydrolyze the substrates, albeit at reduced levels (Figure 4).
Hydrolysis rates for each variant were then calculated using a glucose standard calibration curve.

The procedures applied for determining enzyme activity, using 4-nitrophenyl-O-β-D-glucoside (pNPG, Sigma–Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA), have been previously described [7] and the results are shown in File S5.

This hypothesis has been confirmed by experiments involving random mutagenesis close to W373K, and the identification of a variant (P372T/W373K/M376L) with partially rescued catalytic activity and changed specificity [8].

To examine effects of the substitutions on the pH optimum we analyzed activities of the most and least active variants (W373H and W373K) in relation to pH.

In addition, replacement of Zm-p60.1's hydrophobic cluster with the Brassicaceae consensus cluster (F193A/F200K/W373K/F461L) leads to extensive structural alterations and almost complete abolition of enzymatic activity [7].
For example, mutation W373K, inspired by the composition of the homologous enzyme Bgl4:1 in Brassica napus, severely impairs catalytic activity [7,11].

We found no significant difference between the W373K mutant and WT pH profiles, but the pH optimum of the W373H mutant shifted from 5.5 to 6.
Mutagenesis by NNM codon degeneration yields 32 codon variants, encoding 18 amino acids and 2 stop codons.
A set of 168 β-glycosidase-encoding DNA sequences was selected, as previously described [14], and translated into amino acid sequences.

In the course of saturation mutagenesis at the W373 position of $\beta$ -glucosidase Zm-p60.1 all expressed variants were tested for their ability to cleave the natural substrate tZOG and artificial substrate pNPG.
These results suggest that mutagenesis at the 373 position can influence the optimum pH of Zm-p60.1, but this effect alone is not the cause of the activity reductions.

A simple calculation of the combined probability even for the most frequent amino acids $(3/32)^{21}$ shows that it is highly improbable that all of the positive clones we obtained represented a single variant, and even the negative clone could have had a stop codon at the varied site.
However, most clones proved to be active, for example 20 of 21 clones initially obtained displayed various levels of $\beta$ -glucosidase activity.
Theoretically, there will be 95% probability that an NNK-generated library includes all possible amino acid variants when it contains 163 clones.
Theoretically, there will be 95% probability that an NNK-generated library includes all possible amino acid variants when it contains 171 clones.
Theoretically, there will be 95% probability that an NNN-generated library includes all possible amino acid variants when it contains 240 clones.

Therefore, we applied saturation mutagenesis followed by activity screening to identify other amino acid residues at the W373 position that could be compatible with the composition of $\beta$ -glucosidase Zm-p60.1 and catalytic effects of the substitutions.

The simulation used to calculate theoretical average numbers of clones that needed to be screened for a new hit using each codon degeneration strategy was based on Monte Carlo method (scripts are enclosed as a File S6) implemented in Python 2.7.

Thus, although their catalytic activity was significantly impaired, we have demonstrated that variation at position 373 is possible, without complete loss of activity, and that variants with mutations at the position could be used in efforts to modulate and explain $\beta$ -glucosidases' specificity.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: How to efficiently construct a complete protein library at a highly conserved position when saturation mutagenesis unexpectedly yields many active variants, requiring a combined rational and random strategy.

canonical_events.summary: Hypothesis that W373 is critical, so saturation mutagenesis will yield few active variants.
Design saturation mutagenesis library at W373 using NNM codon degeneration to exclude Met and Trp.
Low transformation efficiency results in few colonies, limiting initial library size.
Screen 21 clones for activity on XGlc plates; 20 are active, 1 inactive due to unintended stop codon.
Sequencing reveals 10 unique amino acid variants at position 373, with frequencies matching codon usage.
key_numbers: Leu (5), Pro (3), Arg (3), Ser (2), Ala (2), Gly (2), His (1), Thr (1), Asp (1), Phe (1)

canonical_events.summary: Monte Carlo simulation shows sharp increase in clones needed to find new variants for last quarter of library.
Cost analysis: SDM ~100 USD/variant vs. screening ~20 USD/variant; SDM becomes cost-effective for missing variants.
Propose combined strategy: random mutagenesis (NNM) plus site-directed mutagenesis to generate missing variants.
Create missing variants (Cys, Glu, Ile, Met, Asn, Gln, Val, Tyr) by individual SDM to complete library.
Expression problems with Leu and Ser variants require codon optimization (TCC for Ser, CTG for Leu).
All expressed variants (18 amino acids) are active on natural substrate tZOG and artificial pNPG, but at reduced levels.
pH optimum analysis of most (W373H) and least (W373K) active variants shows shift for W373H.
All variants have significantly reduced activity compared to wild type; variation tolerated but not optimal.

edges.reason: Hypothesis motivates design of saturation mutagenesis library.
Library construction leads to low transformation efficiency.
Despite low efficiency, screening of obtained clones is performed.

Observed variant frequencies inform simulation of screening requirements.
Simulation results motivate cost analysis of strategies.
Cost analysis supports proposal of combined strategy.
Combined strategy is implemented by creating missing variants via SDM.
SDM reveals expression problems with Leu and Ser variants.
Codon optimization allows expression and validation of all variants.
Further characterization of selected variants' pH optima.
Activity data confirms all variants are active but impaired, highlighting limitation.

must_cover_points: Initial hypothesis that W373 is critical and few active variants expected.
Design of NNM saturation mutagenesis library.
Unexpected high fraction of active clones (20/21).
Sequencing results showing 10 variants with codon bias.
Monte Carlo simulation demonstrating inefficiency of random screening for complete coverage.
Cost analysis favoring SDM for missing variants.
Proposal and implementation of combined random and rational strategy.
Completion of library with all 18 amino acid variants.
Validation that all variants are active but with reduced activity.
Limitation that variation is tolerated but not optimal.
